coredata.dc:description: Excessive exposure to UV and high-energy blue light (HEBL) can severely damage the skin and eyes. Therefore, it is essential to protect our bodies from UV and HEBL radiation. We created PEG-derived carbon dots (PCDs) using the acoustic cavitation approach in order to completely block UV and HEBL. The influence of different experimental parameters such as sonication time, amplitude, and temperature on the PCDs is examined. The optimized PCDs are found to have an average diameter ranging from 5 to 9 nm, contingent upon the preparation circumstances, and to have a quantum yield (QY) of 14–16 %. The PCDs vivid colour and simultaneous UV and HEBL absorption properties allowed the PVA film containing 0.120 wt% of PCDs to demonstrate outstanding blocking efficiency, with 99.9 % blocking in HEBL and 100 % blocking in UV-C, UV-B, and UV-A. The photoluminescence (PL) behaviour of PCDs is greatly reduced by Fe3+ ions because of strong excited state electron transfer. This fluorescent “turn-off” behaviour towards Fe3+ is highly selective and sensitive with a limit of detection (LoD) of 0.366 μM. Paper-based sensors incorporating PCDs are developed for the rapid and accurate detection of Fe3+ in surface water, wastewater treatment plant effluent, and tap water, leveraging their excellent sensitivity and selectivity towards Fe3+. The characteristics of ink-free patterned substrates are displayed by PCDs scattered across a PVA matrix, which makes them useful for the non-destructive acquisition and recognition of latent fingerprints (LFPs). A flexible, transparent film is produced when a LFP is exposed to a PCDs@PVA solution. In this film, a steady luminous fingerprint with specific ridge characteristics that aid in personal recognition is seen. This method shows promise for lifting and identifying long-exposed LFPs from various surfaces without causing damage. It is believed that the interfacial segregation of PCDs inside the PVA matrix during the film production process serves as a mechanism for LFP collection and visualization. The YOLOv8x program, which utilizes deep convolutional neural networks, is employed to analyze the discernible features present in fingerprints (FPs). Notably, their exceptional flexibility, foldability, and sustainability create new opportunities for use in the anti-counterfeiting (AC) field. Because of its remarkable fluorescence stability, it may be a viable substitute for conventional fluorescent inks. Fresh green apples are observed over time after being coated with 0.120 wt% PCDs@PVA and PVA. Virtual assessments indicated that the 0.120 wt% PCDs@PVA film coating significantly reduced weight and moisture loss, effectively inhibiting fungal growth and spoilage for over 25 days at room temperature (RT). Overall, the results clearly show that the PCDs@PVA film is easy to make, flexible, safe for the environment, and resistant to photodegradation. Hemolysis, and blood clotting exhibited favorable biocompatibility and nontoxicity.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Excessive exposure to UV and high-energy blue light (HEBL) can severely damage the skin and eyes. Therefore, it is essential to protect our bodies from UV and HEBL radiation. We created PEG-derived carbon dots (PCDs) using the acoustic cavitation approach in order to completely block UV and HEBL. The influence of different experimental parameters such as sonication time, amplitude, and temperature on the PCDs is examined. The optimized PCDs are found to have an average diameter ranging from 5 to 9 nm, contingent upon the preparation circumstances, and to have a quantum yield (QY) of 14–16 %. The PCDs vivid colour and simultaneous UV and HEBL absorption properties allowed the PVA film containing 0.120 wt% of PCDs to demonstrate outstanding blocking efficiency, with 99.9 % blocking in HEBL and 100 % blocking in UV-C, UV-B, and UV-A. The photoluminescence (PL) behaviour of PCDs is greatly reduced by Fe3+ ions because of strong excited state electron transfer. This fluorescent “turn-off” behaviour towards Fe3+ is highly selective and sensitive with a limit of detection (LoD) of 0.366 μM. Paper-based sensors incorporating PCDs are developed for the rapid and accurate detection of Fe3+ in surface water, wastewater treatment plant effluent, and tap water, leveraging their excellent sensitivity and selectivity towards Fe3+. The characteristics of ink-free patterned substrates are displayed by PCDs scattered across a PVA matrix, which makes them useful for the non-destructive acquisition and recognition of latent fingerprints (LFPs). A flexible, transparent film is produced when a LFP is exposed to a PCDs@PVA solution. In this film, a steady luminous fingerprint with specific ridge characteristics that aid in personal recognition is seen. This method shows promise for lifting and identifying long-exposed LFPs from various surfaces without causing damage. It is believed that the interfacial segregation of PCDs inside the PVA matrix during the film production process serves as a mechanism for LFP collection and visualization. The YOLOv8x program, which utilizes deep convolutional neural networks, is employed to analyze the discernible features present in fingerprints (FPs). Notably, their exceptional flexibility, foldability, and sustainability create new opportunities for use in the anti-counterfeiting (AC) field. Because of its remarkable fluorescence stability, it may be a viable substitute for conventional fluorescent inks. Fresh green apples are observed over time after being coated with 0.120 wt% PCDs@PVA and PVA. Virtual assessments indicated that the 0.120 wt% PCDs@PVA film coating significantly reduced weight and moisture loss, effectively inhibiting fungal growth and spoilage for over 25 days at room temperature (RT). Overall, the results clearly show that the PCDs@PVA film is easy to make, flexible, safe for the environment, and resistant to photodegradation. Hemolysis, and blood clotting exhibited favorable biocompatibility and nontoxicity.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para:
The synthesis procedure assisted by acoustic cavitation holds indispensable applications in organic synthesis, organometallic chemistry, nanomaterials, and industrial manufacturing processes [1]. Ultrasound power triggers acoustic cavitation, the key mechanism responsible for the formation, nucleation, and eventual collapse of bubbles within an aqueous solution. Aqueous solution bubbles collapse, producing a great deal of heat energy and pressure quickly. Acoustic wavelengths are usually between 10 cm and 100 mm, and they are accompanied by frequencies between 15 kHz and 10 MHz. According to the reported literature, cavitation implosion produces high temperatures (>5000 K) and high pressures (>20 MPa), together with a fast-cooling rate of ∼105 K/s [2]. Acoustic waves, often referred to as shock waves, accelerate inter-particle friction. This friction between particles contributes to the weakening of precursor materials, leading to the formation of nano sized particles. A schematic representation of ultrasound-assisted PCDs development is shown in Fig. 1
. The following is an outline of the traditional sonochemical approach for PCDs preparation: (1) A certain amount of molecule containing carbon is dissolved in an appropriate solvent, typically water. (2) Next, this solution is subjected to ultrasonic waves for a predetermined amount of time. (3) Reactive H· and OH· radicals are produced when water is exposed to ultrasonic radiation [3], which are essential for the synthesis of PCDs. (4) As a result of these reactive radicals' interactions with the solution carbon precursor, functionalized PCDs are created. The following is an overview of the potential mechanisms (including competing reactions) for PCD creation via the acoustic cavitation-assisted synthesis route [4]:
(1)
H 2
O → H · + O H ·
H · + H · →
H 2
H · + O H · →
H 2
O
OH · + O H · →
H 2
O 2
R H
Carbon
p r e c u r s o r
→ R · + H ·
R · + O H · → F u n c t i o n a l i z e d
P C D s

Li et. al [5] prepared PCDs with an exceptionally narrow particle size distribution by treating glucose sono-chemically in either an acid or alkali medium. At that time, the QY of these PCDs is 7 %. Huang and collaborators utilized the acoustic cavitation-assisted method to produce photoluminescent carbon nanodots that are functionalized with polyethylene glycol containing −SH end groups [6]. Methoxy polyethylene glycol gave the PCDs exceptional biocompatibility in addition to increasing their water dispersibility. The resultant PCDs showed excellent bioimaging capability with low cytotoxicity. This method opens the door to the development of polymer-functionalized PCDs with adaptable attributes. To build on this, Kumar et al., [7] created a variety of doped PCDs containing different metals, including tin, silver, gold, and gallium, using an ultrasound-assisted synthesis process that did not require the use of a catalyst or stabilizer. Blueberries are sonicated to create fluorescent PCDs, which Aslandas et al., [8] then used to detect Fe3+ ions in water solutions ranging from 12.5-100 µM. Qiang et al., [9] used ultrasound-assisted synthesis procedures to produce vivid green luminous PCDs from potatoes. For the sensitive and targeted detection of Zn2+ in aquatic conditions, these produced PCDs function as an efficient photoluminescent sensing probe.

Kumar et al. [7] developed a sonochemical method that is economical. This method involved ultrasonically irradiating PEG-400 solvent, followed by its breakdown, to generate PCDs in situ. Metallic tin is added to the reaction chamber and heated to its melting point (507 K) in the presence of PEG-400. The resulting dual-phase mixture is then sonicated to create Sn@Carbon dots [10]. Tangy et al. [11] utilized sonication to synthesize carbon dots from PEG-400. Subsequently, they employed these CDs for the sonochemical modification of strontium nitrate, a precursor for strontium oxide. The ultrasonic irradiation facilitated the attachment of CDs to the outer surface of strontium nitrate. Following this, the resulting material is subjected to calcination in an inert environment, resulting in the formation of a NCs consisting of strontium oxide and CDs [12]. Zhuo et al., [13] utilized the ultrasonic method to synthesize graphene quantum dots. The procedure commenced with the oxidation of graphene in concentrated nitric acid and sulfuric acid solutions at room temperature, lasting for 12 h. Subsequently, the solution mixture underwent ultrasonic irradiation for an additional 12 h. The ensuing mixture is subsequently subjected to calcination at 623 K for 20 min, and the resulting products are dispersed in water. Following this, the solution undergoes dialysis to produce graphene quantum dots. Sharma et al., [14] employed sonochemical techniques to synthesize an α-Bi2O3/CDs nanocomposite. This photocatalyst demonstrated exceptional visible-light-driven photocatalytic activity in decomposing indigo carmine dye. It achieved 86 % dye degradation in just 120 min, surpassing the 57 % achieved by pure α-Bi2O3. Furthermore, within the same timeframe, the novel α-Bi2O3/CDs composite showcased a 79 % degradation rate of the antibiotic drug levofloxacin. Using ultrasonic waves at ambient temperature, Park et al., [5,15] developed a unique large-scale synthesis technique for CDs produced from food waste. These CDs showed excellent photostability, consistent size and shape, low cytotoxicity, and outstanding fluorescence qualities, which made them ideal for biomedical applications. The importance of sonication factors in CDs fabrication methods must be emphasized.

CDs, classified as a form of carbon-based nanomaterials, possess numerous advantageous traits such as customizable PL, high water solubility, low toxicity, cost-effective and excellent biocompatibility preparation methods. Since their initial discovery in 2004, these nanomaterials have attracted increasing attention and have emerged as promising candidates for a range of applications, including bioimaging, optoelectronic devices, sensing, and catalysis. The synthesis of CDs has advanced significantly, requiring the investigation of several precursors, including graphite, citric acid or its salts, phenylenediamines, eggs, glass, and plant leaves. Many synthetic pathways, such as pyrolysis, plasma treatments, and electrochemical, microwave, and hydrothermal synthesis, have been devised. Moreover, there has been an attempt to increase the volume of CDs produced. The development of CDs for commercial use has been prompted by notable advancements in their availability; yet, widespread commercial use has not yet been fully realized. The significant advancements in CDs have not been fully shown because of the practical uses of CDs that are still underutilized in a few critical industrial sectors. Consequently, it is critical to identify workable routes that allow CDs to demonstrate broad commercial applicability.

Fe3+ ions are essential for all living organisms, playing a crucial role in various metabolic processes such as oxygen transport, nucleic acid synthesis, and electron transport. Additionally, these ions are necessary for living cells to maintain their function, metabolism, and proliferation during infection. However, when Fe3+ levels exceed physiological thresholds, they can negatively impact cellular structures and functions across various organs, including the heart, pancreas, kidneys, and liver. Iron overload is also associated with impaired bone marrow function and an increased risk of cancer. Although excessive Fe3+ accumulation in humans is often linked to the consumption of contaminated food products [16,17], its origins can be traced to the leaching of iron from the weathering of iron silicates and other minerals into aquatic environments, where it appears as hydrated iron oxide. Ferric ions are also toxic to fish and other aquatic life, making it essential to develop rapid monitoring techniques for detecting their presence, even at very low concentrations in natural waters. Among the existing methods, such as spectrophotometry, flow-injection analysis, graphite furnace atomic absorption, and high-resolution electrospray ionization mass spectrometry, fluorescence-based techniques are considered the most suitable due to their speed, high sensitivity, selectivity, and the absence of complex pre-treatment steps. Although various “turn-on” fluorescent probes for Fe3+ detection are available, recent studies have increasingly focused on using carbon quantum dots as “turn-off” sensors for detecting various metal ions, including Fe3+ [18–20].

Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) is known for its highwater solubility and hydrophilicity, thanks to the numerous hydroxyl groups in its molecular structure. It also possesses advantageous characteristics such as compatibility, biodegradability, thermal and processability properties. Therefore, PVA has become a renewable polymer sourced from bio-based materials, and its applications span across packaging, biomedicine, food processing, adhesives and construction industries [21–25]. Despite its beneficial properties, it's important to highlight that PVA is flammable, having a limited oxygen index value (LOI) of 19.5 %. The flame propagation of PVA can vary depending on its shape and thickness when ignited [26]. Usually, polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) demonstrates an initial decomposition temperature at approximately 200 °C and reaches its maximum decomposition temperature around 300 °C. When subjected to an ignition source, the polymer chains within PVA degrade rapidly, leading to the generation of small molecules. This degradation process occurs due to the breaking of chemical bonds within the polymer structure, resulting in the release of volatile components. This process leads to intense burning and melt dripping, leaving minimal residual char [27,28]. Currently, there is significant research interest in improving the flame retardancy of PVA.

In this context, we introduce a previously unexplored benefit of CDs, CD/polymer composites have the capability to serve as ink-free patterned substrate films, which are excellent for capturing and identifying LFPs. These composites also hold promise for various applications within the printing sector. FP identification finds widespread application across various fields including personal authentication, access control, and forensic analysis. The pattern formed by the ridges and furrows on a human finger is distinct for each person, yet its contact imprint, termed a LFP, remains unseen without specialized techniques. A range of advanced techniques has been devised to visualize LFPs, integrating physical, chemical, spectroscopic, and hybrid methodologies. Recent research indicates that CDs could offer a safe and effective alternative as fingerprinting ink and for LFP recovery due to their low toxicity. Although CDs show promise for fingerprinting applications, challenges such as the susceptibility of FP patterns to damage and a notable decrease in fluorescence with CD-based dusting techniques still need to be addressed. Therefore, there is ongoing research aimed at developing straightforward approaches for effective LFP recognition. While our team and other researchers have successfully generated fluorescence patterns using CDs as ink through methods like handwriting, inkjet printing, or silk-screen printing, the potential of CD composites as ink-free patterned substrates in the printing industry remains unexplored.

The proliferation of counterfeit goods poses a significant challenge, encompassing the mass production of forged and substandard items such as certificates, passports, diplomas, and branded products [29]. The problem poses risks to property security, societal stability, and public health, with estimations suggesting that the manufacturing and distribution of counterfeit goods have resulted in billions of dollars in profits [30]. The counterfeit market has undergone significant growth, expanding at a notable rate of 14.0 % and reaching a substantial value of $206 billion by 2021 [31]. As a result, there is an urgent need to advance sophisticated high-security anti-counterfeiting techniques. These techniques are crucial for enhancing document security and effectively discouraging both forgery and counterfeiting activities. Numerous anti-counterfeiting technologies have been developed to combat this issue, encompassing a range of strategies. These include luminescent anti-counterfeiting materials [32], AC labels [33], laser AC methods [34], and computer security techniques [35]. Each of these technologies employs distinct mechanisms and approaches to enhance authentication and thwart the proliferation of counterfeit goods. Among these technologies, luminescent AC materials have garnered considerable global attention [36]. These techniques enable the creation of intricate, multi-coloured anti-counterfeiting patterns using a variety of visible colours. This is accomplished through the incorporation of fluorescent dyes, luminescent materials containing lanthanide ions, and CDs, resulting in the development of sophisticated designs [37]. Current luminescent anti-counterfeiting methods mainly involve printing luminescent patterns onto paper. However, this method is prone to structural imperfections and mechanical damage during manufacturing, resulting in compromised quality and a restricted lifespan [38,39]. Therefore, there is an urgent requirement to develop smart luminescent materials that combine strong fluorescence with mechanical durability to enhance AC capabilities.

UV radiation, situated between visible light and X-rays on the electromagnetic spectrum, is typically categorized into three distinct ranges based on wavelength: UV-A (315–400 nm), UV-B (280–315 nm), and UV-C (200–280 nm) [40]. These different categories of UV radiation have varying effects on biological organisms and materials, with UV-A being responsible for tanning and aging of the skin, UV-B causing sunburn and contributing to skin cancer risk, and UV-C possessing germicidal properties often used for disinfection purposes [41]. Understanding these distinctions is crucial for various applications ranging from healthcare to environmental protection. The UV radiation naturally emitted by the sun plays a substantial role in human health. Exposure to moderate levels of UV radiation contributes positively to the production of vitamin D, yet excessive exposure can be detrimental, leading to skin and eye issues such as photoaging, skin cancer, cataracts, and macular degeneration [42,43]. UV-B radiation is recognized for its ability to trigger the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), which in turn can cause oxidative stress, apoptosis and cellular damage [44–46]. Furthermore, the widespread use of electronic devices such as tablets, smartphones and LED televisions has resulted in increased exposure to HEBL [47]. HEBL, distinguished by its shortest and highest-energy wavelengths within the visible spectrum, typically falls within the range of 400 to 450 nm [48,49]. Overexposure to HEBL disturbs sleep patterns by suppressing melatonin secretion, a hormone essential for inducing sleep. Additionally, it can damage the retina, possibly leading to increased production of ROS and the loss of photoreceptor and retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) cells, ultimately resulting in apoptosis [50,51]. To tackle these issues, it is essential to develop a film that selectively filters UV and HEBL rays while preserving transparency to visible light.

In this study, PCDs@PVA films are produced to efficiently block UV and HEBL ranges. PCDs are nano-sized spherical particles with diameters less than 10 nm, known for their fluorescence emission properties. They have low toxicity, high biocompatibility, photostability, and can be readily modified with different functional groups on their surfaces [52,53]. The favorable characteristics of CDs have contributed to their increasing use in various domains, including drug delivery, bioimaging, biosensors, nanozyme and antibacterial applications [54,55]. Our breakthrough marks a significant step forward in creating films based on PCDs that can effectively block both UV and HEBL. This means that PCDs can absorb both UV and HEBL simultaneously. Moreover, by harnessing these optical features, we developed a PVA film integrated with PCDs using a simple solution casting method. The resulting PCDs@PVA film exhibited enhanced optical clarity throughout the visible light spectrum, simultaneously providing effective blocking of both UV and HEBL. This advancement is particularly significant as it ensures the transmission of visible light while offering protection against harmful UV and HEBL wavelengths. Additionally, we suggest a mechanism to visualize LFPs involving the segregation of PCDs at the interface within the polymer matrix during film formation. Remarkably, relief patterns or groove images from diverse surfaces can be effortlessly transferred onto the PCDs@PVA NC films without the need for ink. Moreover, these composite films can serve as fluorescent ink for screen printing. Hence, we present a versatile platform that enables the integration of PCDs into various applications, including FP identification, robust fluorescence printing and AC labelling.

PCDs integrated into a PVA polymer matrix function as an edible and UV-protective coating designed to extend the shelf life of perishable foods like green apples. Comprehensive assessments of the optical and thermal attributes of the resultant NC films are carried out. The creation of PCDs@PVA NC films is undertaken to maximize their potential as UV barriers in food packaging. Furthermore, paper-based sensors employing PCDs to detect Fe3+ are developed, facilitating quick and straightforward testing across different water samples, such as surface water, wastewater treatment plant effluent, and tap water.
PEG-400 (99.99 % purity) is obtained from Sigma-Aldrich and used as received without any additional modification.

A test tube containing 12 mm of PEG-400 is submerged in a water/Si-oil bath that is heated to a temperature between 4 and 150 °C. After that, an ultrasonic transducer tip that is positioned roughly 1 cm above the test tube's bottom is immersed in the solution. An ultrasonic transducer (Sonics and Materials Inc., USA, model VCX 750, frequency 20 kHz, voltage 230 V AC) is used to apply ultrasonic irradiation. Throughout the procedure, the temperature of the water/Si-oil bath (4–150 °C), reaction amplitude (30–60 %), time (2, 3 and 4 h) and temperature (30–90 °C) are all regulated appropriately. After the reaction solution naturally cooled to room temperature, contaminants are removed by dialysis with deionized water for a full day. Next, lyophilization is applied to the dialysis bag's aqueous solution to produce powdered PCDs. Fig. 1a & b show the schematic diagram that shows how PCDs are prepared and how different sonochemical parameters are adjusted, such as temperature (°C), amplitude (%), and sonication time (h). Further, blue, green and red-emitting PCDs are synthesized using water, glycerol, and toluene as solvents, respectively, through an ultrasonication method. In this process, a solution of carbon precursors is prepared in each solvent: distilled water for blue-emitting PCDs, glycerol for green-emitting PCDs, and toluene for red-emitting PCDs. Fig. S1 The schematic representation of multi-colour emitting PCDs via ultrasonication.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing of Fe3+ ions in ideal and real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the impact of various metal ions (Fe3+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Ag+, and Na+) on the fluorescence behavior of PCDs, 1 mL of the PCD solution is mixed with 1 mL of 500 μM solutions of each metal ion, along with a blank sample. The fluorescent intensities are measured after allowing the mixtures to equilibrate for 15 min. For the PCDs-Fe3+ mixtures, the same procedure is repeated, with final Fe3+ concentrations ranging from 0 to 250 μM. To assess the applicability of this method in real-world scenarios, river water collected locally, deionized water from a chemistry laboratory, and tap water are spiked with 25 μM ferric ions, equilibrated with the PCDs, and their effects on fluorescence are subsequently studied.

The dispersing agent is essential for two primary reasons when making the anti-counterfeiting ink with PVA and PCDs: viscosity and dispersibility. By mixing the optimized PCDs in solution uniformly, dispersibility assures transparency, while viscosity adds to the adhesive quality needed for printing on paper. Using a magnetic stirrer, thoroughly mix 30 mL of deionized (DI) water while gradually dissolving PVA until a transparent solution develops. After that, PCDs are disintegrated in the mixture to produce the clear, transparent security ink.

ce:list-item.ce:label: a.
ce:list-item.ce:para: Screen-print mode: The FL security ink containing PCDs is applied to one end of the printing screen plate, and pressure is applied using a squeegee on the ink area of the plate. While the squeegee moves, the ink transfers from the mesh of the patterned section onto the substrate, facilitating the printing of diverse patterns on surfaces crafted from a range of materials. Upon curing, the patterns emit a steady, vivid blue light at a wavelength of 365 nm. Despite this luminosity, they maintain transparency and remain undetectable in daylight, highlighting robust concealment attributes.

ce:list-item.ce:label: b.
ce:list-item.ce:para: Sketch pen mode: The PVA solution is sonicated to disperse the right amounts of PCDs, creating the anti-counterfeiting marker. Subsequently, this ink is utilized to create anti-counterfeiting labels on different surfaces through the sketch pen technique. This method involves manually drawing or tracing the desired designs or patterns using the ink, effectively integrating anti-counterfeiting features into the labels.

ce:list-item.ce:label: c.
ce:list-item.ce:para: Simple brush mode: The homogeneous blend serves as fluorescent ink suitable for writing, drawing, and printing purposes. Text and images are created by applying the PCDs solution with a brush on non-fluorescent paper. Photos of the paper are taken using a Canon EOS 1500D Digital SLR Camera (EF S18-55 Lens) under both daylight and 365 nm UV light. Fig. 2 a illustrates the development of anti-counterfeiting patterns using different techniques.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of photoluminescent PVA and PCDs@PVA nanocomposite films
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent PCDs@PVA NC films are produced by blending different concentrations (0.015 to 0.120 wt%) of PCDs, exhibiting vibrant blue emissions, into a PVA suspension. Initially, 2 g of PVA is dispersed in 20 mL of deionized water and heated to 90 °C with vigorous stirring using a magnetic stirrer for about 25 min until a clear solution formed. The solution is then cooled to RT (25 °C). Afterward, 1 mL of PCDs solutions at designated mass concentrations (0.015 to 0.120 wt%) are added to the PVA solution. The mixture underwent several cycles of stirring and sonication for 20 min until a uniform solution (Fig. 2b) is obtained. Then, 10 mL of the resulting PVA or PCDs@PVA solutions are poured into a glass petri dish using a micropipette and allowed to dry at RT for 2 days to facilitate film formation. The process of film formation entails regulating the evaporation of water. To guarantee homogeneity, the same process is used to each PCDs@PVA NC film sample. Throughout the preparation procedure, the film diameters stayed constant and the same amount of PCDs@PVA solution is used to maintain consistent film thickness. A tiny digital thickness gauge yielded an approximate thickness of 100 μm for the casted films. PVA, 0.015 wt% PCDs@PVA, 0.045 wt% PCDs@PVA, 0.075 wt% PCDs@PVA, and 0.120 wt% PCDs@PVA are the labels applied to the samples. After that, they are kept for a full year in a stability chamber that is kept at 25 °C and 75 % relative humidity (RH) in the dark. The film structure and optical characteristics are evaluated on a regular basis during this time. The schematic for the production of PCDs and mechanism of PCDs and PVA films for UV-blocking is shown in Fig. S2 a.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of PCDs@PVA NC as ink-free patterned substrates
ce:section.ce:para: 20 g of PVA solid and one hundred grams of deionized water are combined in a flask with three necks and a circular bottom that had a stirrer attached. After heating the mixture to 90 °C, it is stirred for 1 h. The mixture is then mixed with a 10 g aqueous PCD solution (containing 0.01 g of PCDs per mm). An aqueous solution of PCDs@PVA composites is produced after stirring for 20 min. A surface bearing a pattern or a LFPs is drop-cast with this solution. After that, the solvents are let to evaporate, which produced a clear, flexible film. The resulting film is delicately lifted off the surface, revealing a durable fluorescent FP or pattern on its surface (Fig. 2c). A similar method is used to create luminescent hydrogels, with the detailed fabrication steps discussed elsewhere [56]. The schematic diagram illustrating the preparation of luminescent hydrogels is shown in Fig. 2d. Schematic representation of mechanism of luminescent hydrogel Fig. S2b.

First, 5 mg of PCDs is dissolved in 100 mL of water, and the resulting solution is allowed to soak into circular filter paper for 25 min. Subsequently, the filter paper is removed and allowed to dry naturally. During this process, the paper is left undisturbed to air dry, ensuring that no external forces affected the drying procedure. After that, the dry filter paper is immersed for roughly 5 sec in solutions containing varying amounts of Fe3+. Following this, the test paper underwent exposure to a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp, and a reference card is generated for Fe3+ concentration based on the fluorescence colour exhibited by the test paper. The procedure for fabricating the paper-based sensor is illustrated in Fig. 2e. This step involved utilizing the fluorescence response of the test paper to Fe3+ ions under UV light to establish a quantitative reference scale for concentration analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Coating of green apples with PVA and PCDs@PVA NCs
ce:section.ce:para: Fresh green apples are acquired from a grocery store in Tumkur, Karnataka, ensuring uniformity in size, colour, shape, texture, and absence of visible marks or decay prior to any coatings being applied. The apples are first washed and dried as part of the initial preparation process. The cleaned apples are then dipped twice into solutions containing PVA, PCDs and (0.120 wt%)PCDs@PVA via the dip-coating technique. Images of the coated green apples are taken with a Nikon camera over a period of 0–25 days for visual evaluation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The UV-A/B/C and HEBL blocking capabilities of PVA film and PCDs@PVA NC films
ce:section.ce:para:
Using UV–vis spectrophotometry, the absorption capacities of both UV and high-energy blue light (HEBL) for each PCDs@PVA film are assessed. The effectiveness of the PCDs@PVA films' UV and HEBL blocking is assessed using optical transmittance. The UV and HEBL protection rates of these films are determined through the application of the provided equation. Additionally, the UV-A (315–400 nm) blocking percentage of each film is calculated using Eq. (1). This assessment enabled the quantification of the NC films effectiveness in blocking UV radiation and high-energy blue light, providing valuable insights into their protective properties against these potentially harmful wavelengths [57].
