The Methyl Thiazolyl Tetrazolium (MTT) assay is conducted to evaluate the cell viability of PCDs against Neuroblastoma (SH-SY-5Y) cells. The cells are incubated in a Minimum Essential Medium (MEM) supplemented with fetal bovine serum (FBS) at 37 °C in a 5 % CO2 atmosphere. After a 24 h incubation period, the SH-SY-5Y cells are treated with various concentrations of the PCDs solution (0.5, 2.5, 5, 7.5, and 10 µM). To ensure proper conditions, the cells are thoroughly washed with Phosphate Buffered Saline (PBS) at a pH of 7.2. Subsequently, MTT (1 mg/mL) is added to each cell well, and the cells are incubated for an additional 3 h. After this incubation period, the culture medium is carefully removed. To dissolve the resulting crystallized formazan, 100 % Dimethyl Sulfoxide (DMSO) is added. The optical absorbance of each solution is then measured using inverted phase contrast microscopy at 570 nm, allowing for the estimation of cell viability using the following equation [58]:
Cellviability =
OD
e
OD
c
× 100 %
where ODe represents the absorbance of the experimental group, and ODc denotes the absorbance of the control group. The IC50 value, indicating the concentration at which 50 % of cells remain viable, was calculated using linear regression (Y = mx + c, with Y set to 50 and m and c as derived values).

A Shimadzu 7000 powder X-ray diffractometer is used to do the X-ray diffraction investigation. The diffractometer is set to scan at a step rate of 0.02°/min between 10 and 60°. A confocal RAMAN spectrometer model 500 (STR 500) is utilized to perform Raman spectroscopy, with an excitation laser wavelength of 532 nm. The samples' fracture surface morphology is investigated using a Hitachi SU-8010 FE-SEM running at 10 kV and 8 mA. The sample is fastened in place using the sample stub. The sample is coated for 30 sec with carbon and then with gold to reduce static charge. PerkinElmer Frontier equipment (PerkinElmer, Inc., USA) is used to obtain FTIR spectra. The sample is first dried at 50 °C in an oven until its weight stabilized, which is then checked on a regular basis. The dried sample is then scanned at frequencies between 4000–400 cm−1. A 450 W Xenon lamp-equipped Horiba spectrofluorimeter is used to obtain the fluorescence spectra. Using a photometric integrating sphere-assisted technique and transient steady-state spectroscopy (FLS1000, Edinburgh Instruments, UK), the fluorescence QY of CDs is ascertained. A 200 kV accelerating voltage is used to perform analysis with a JEOL 2100 microscope for high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). A droplet of isopropanol solution containing PEG and CDs is dried on a copper grid coated with carbon to create the TEM samples. HRTEM Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDS) is used to perform elemental mapping and elemental analysis. The materials are subjected to X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) investigations utilizing an ESCALAB MKII spectrometer and a monochromatic X-ray source with Al Kα excitation (1486.6 eV). Calibration of binding energy is carried out using C1s at 285 eV. With the use of a Shimadzu (Kyoto, Japan) thermal analysis instrument, the samples thermal characteristics (TGA and DTG) are assessed. The TA-60WS thermal analyzer, FC-60A flow controller, and TA-60 software are included with this instrument. The sample is introduced into the instrument with a nitrogen flow rate of 50 mL/min and a heating rate of 20 °C/min. The tensile properties of all samples are evaluated for significance using variance analysis (ANOVA). Differences are analyzed using Duncan's multiple range test and are considered significant at a p-value of 0.05.

The diffraction pattern of the carbon PCDs synthesized with a sonication time of 3 h, amplitude of 60 %, and temperature of 90 °C is illustrated in Fig. 3 a-c. The peaks detected at 2θ = 19.87–20.51° and 40.49–41.13° are attributed to the (002) and (100) planes, respectively, of carbonaceous materials [59]. These peaks indicate the crystalline structure of the carbon-based substances, providing insights into their molecular arrangement and composition. The presence of the (002) plane is associated with the organization of disordered carbon atoms within the hexagonal graphite structure of carbon quantum dots. This arrangement leads to the development of a turbostratic structure through interlayer packing, as documented by the JCPDS No. 26–1076 [60]. These structural characteristic sheds light on the unique arrangement of carbon atoms within the quantum dots, offering valuable insights into their properties and potential applications. As a result, the PCDs primarily shows a carbon framework characterized by a graphitic structure. This structure is distinguished by interlayer spacings of 0.38 nm and 0.21 nm, which correspond to the (002) and (100) diffraction peaks, respectively [61]. These findings highlight the specific structural properties of the PCDs, providing valuable insights into their composition and potential applications. In contrast to the d-spacing between the (002) planes of conventional bulk graphite, which is approximately 0.34 nm, this spacing is larger. This variance can be attributed to the amorphous nature of the carbon structure. Hence, these two diffraction peaks serve as distinctive signature peaks of carbon, suggesting nanostructures composed predominantly of densely packed carbon and oxygen atoms. Additionally, XRD analysis of PVA and PCDs@PVA NC films is discussed in Note S1 of the supplementary section ( Fig. S3 ). Raman spectroscopy is utilized to evaluate the impact of PCDs prepared under varying experimental parameters, including sonication time (3 h), amplitude (60 %), and temperature (90 °C), on the degree of graphitization. As depicted in Fig. 3d-f, all PCDs displayed two distinct Raman absorption peaks falling within the ranges of 1317–1378 cm−1 and 1582–1593 cm−1, representing disordered carbon (D) and graphitic carbon (G), respectively. Generally, the level of graphitization in the residual char is evaluated by calculating the ratio of the integrated peak area of the D peak to that of the G peak (ID/IG). This ratio provides valuable insights into the structural properties and degree of graphitization of the carbon-based materials. Higher graphitization is indicated by lower ID/IG values [62]. The ID/IG value in the 0.015 wt% PCDs sample is 1.16; adding 0.045–0.120 wt% PCDs caused the ratio to slightly drop to 1.15. Fig. 3g–i display the analysis of the FTIR spectra of PCDs generated under various conditions, such as sonication time (3 h), amplitude (60 %), and temperature (90 °C). Table 1 lists the wavenumbers and the related assignments [63].

As depicted in Fig. 4 a, the full-scan XPS analysis of the optimized 0.120 wt% PCDs reveals characteristic peaks corresponding to O, C, and N at 531.9, 284.6, and 400.2 eV, respectively. Furthermore, Fig. 4b displays the high-resolution XPS map of the O1s. Within this map, the three peaks observed at 531.3 and 532.7 eV are identified as the characteristic peaks of C-O and C = O, respectively [64]. Fig. 4c displays a high-resolution XPS map of the C1s. The peaks observed at 284.3, 285.4, and 287.3 eV correspond to C = C/C–C, C–N, and C = O, respectively [65]. Additionally, the peaks detected in the N 1 s spectrum at 399.7 and 400.8 eV (Fig. 4d) signify the presence of pyridinic nitrogen and pyrrolic nitrogen [66].

PCDs are made over 3 h at a constant temperature of 55 °C, with different sonication amplitudes. Measurements are made of the fluorescence that the PCDs in the supernatant solution released at various excitation wavelengths (330–490 nm). The typical spectra from the PCDs generated at various sonication amplitudes (30–60 %) are shown in Fig. 5 a. The broad bands seen in all emission spectra are centered between 440 and 480 nm. The augmentation in both emission intensity and the area beneath the emission curves becomes apparent with higher sonication amplitudes, escalating up to 60 %. This phenomenon can be attributed to the increased formation of PCDs at higher sonication intensities, which is facilitated by the higher efficient decomposition of PEG. Peak emission intensity is consistently observed at an excitation wavelength of 370 nm across all four scenarios. The most prominent emission fell within the 460–480 nm range, characterized by a relatively narrow FWHM. The narrow FWHM indicates a relatively uniform size distribution of the particles. This observation aligns with the findings from TEM analysis (Fig. 8g-i), confirming that the particles are predominantly uniform in size, with most being smaller than 10 nm.

The emission peaks of PCDs produced at 60 % amplitude and higher, when excited at 370 nm, are observed to be 30 nm higher compared to particles produced at 30 % amplitude. This difference could be linked to an enlargement in the average size of the PCDs, as evidenced in the TEM images (Fig. 8g-i). The TEM images of the PCDs synthesized in PEG-400 at varying amplitudes show that the smaller PCDs exhibit a spherical shape, with their diameters varying based on the sonication amplitude. At 30 % amplitude, the particle diameters ranged from 4-10 nm. At 50 % amplitude, the diameters varied between 3–10 nm. Similarly, at 60 % amplitude, the diameters are also in the range of 4–10 nm. Significantly, at the elevated sonication amplitude of 60 %, slightly larger particles (4–10 nm) are detected. This occurrence can be elucidated as follows: it is widely acknowledged that in the presence of solid materials, the continuous collapse of acoustic bubbles results in the formation of microjets that rapidly move towards the solid particles at extremely high velocities. The small size of the target particles increases the likelihood of collisions, which promotes the coalescence of colliding particles. This effect is amplified at higher sonication amplitudes due to the increased probability of collisions. Additionally, the dissociated liquid in this scenario possesses a comparatively high density. Therefore, increased amplitudes might result in the concurrent dissociation of adjacent molecules, thereby enhancing the formation of larger particles.

To ascertain the minimum sonication time required for PEG to produce small-sized PCDs with strong fluorescence, we conducted several experiments with varying sonication durations: 2, 3, 4 and 5 h. We kept the sonication amplitude at 60 % and started at temperatures of 55 °C. Fig. 5b shows the FL spectra of these products at different excitation wavelengths. As shown in the figure, the sonication duration within this time range has minimal effect on both the excitation and emission wavelengths and their intensities. However, it is important to note that sonication for only 2 h led to a noticeably lower fluorescence intensity. This suggests that the process of PEG decomposition to form PCDs is time-sensitive, with a specific threshold for each condition set, below which only a limited amount of PCDs can be produced. Following the determination of the optimal formation condition, which yielded the highest fluorescence intensity (3 h of sonication at 60 % amplitude), we investigated the impact of the initial sonication temperature under these conditions. PCDs are synthesized at temperatures ranging from 30 to 90 °C, and the fluorescence of each resultant product is assessed. At 5 °C, no fluorescence is detected, while at 25 °C, fluorescence bands of considerably lower intensities are observed compared to those at higher temperatures (Fig. 5c). As the formation temperature increased, higher intensities are recorded, with a distinct rise noted for the sample synthesized at 90 °C. The increased intensity indicates a greater abundance of PCDs in that particular sample, potentially attributed to the decreased viscosity of the PEG at higher temperatures. This decrease enables a more effective cavitation process or a more straightforward decomposition of PEG to generate PCDs.

The investigation also extended to exploring the pH sensitivity of the FL PCDs. Fig. 6f displays a fluorescence emission spectrum of the aqueous solution of PCDs across various pH values. This examination provides insights into how the fluorescence emission of the PCDs is influenced by changes in pH levels, offering valuable information for potential applications in pH sensing and detection. It is evident that the fluorescence intensity of the PCDs varies with the pH value. The emission peak undergoes minimal shifting as the pH ranges from 1 to 14. There is only a slight decrease in fluorescence intensity at pH 14, with no significant quenching effect observed. However, there is a notable decrease in intensity observed at a relatively swift rate within the pH range of 11 to 14. This is evident from the comparative photographs of pH values 1 and 14 inserted in Fig. 6f, highlighting a significant quenching effect. The fluorescence intensity of the CDs seems to be remarkably sensitive to the presence of a strong alkaline solution. Additionally, the prepared PCDs are dissolved in water, methanol, dimethylformamide (DMF), and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) to explore the influence of the solvent on their fluorescent behaviour. The PCDs demonstrate a favorable fluorescent yield when dissolved in water (Fig. 6g), indicating their high solubility in a universal solvent without compromising their fluorescent activity. Furthermore, the most intense fluorescence is noted in DMSO. This could be attributed to the presence of the amide group on the surface of the PCDs.

The fluorescence QY of the prepared PCDs is assessed by comparing the integrated PL intensities, excited at 360 nm, and the absorbance values at the same wavelength with those of quinine sulfate (QS) in 0.2 M H2SO4, which has a known QY of 54 %. This comparison allows for the quantification of the PCDs' fluorescence efficiency relative to a standard reference material, providing insights into their optical properties and potential applications. In particular, absorbance measurements of the PCDs and QS are carried out at 360 nm. PL emission spectra of both the PCDs and QS are recorded with an excitation wavelength of 360 nm to quantify the integrated fluorescence intensity. This measurement encompasses the area under the PL curve within the wavelength range from 350 to 700 nm. This comprehensive analysis allows for the comparison of the fluorescence efficiency of the CDs with that of QS, providing detailed insights into their optical performance. Subsequently, the integrated fluorescence intensity is plotted against the absorbance. The QY values are calculated using the following equation [69]:
(6)
QY =
QY
st
A x
A
st
×
PL
st
PL
x
×
n x
n
st
× 100 %

In this context, the subscripts “st” and “x” indicate the QS standard and the PCDs, respectively. Here, QY denotes the fluorescence quantum yield, A represents absorbance, PL indicates the integrated area under the emission peak, and “n” is the refractive index of the solvent, set at 1.33 for both. This equation allows for the determination of the QY of the PCDs relative to QS, providing quantitative information about their fluorescence efficiency. To mitigate re-absorption effects, the absorbance at 360 nm in a 10 mm cuvette is maintained below 0.1 OD (optical density).

The QY is evaluated under the optimized formation conditions of PCDs, involving 60 % amplitude and 3 h of sonication at 90 °C. The aqueous dispersion of the PCDs in water remained transparent in daylight and exhibited stability for several weeks. When excited with a 360 nm UV lamp, the PCDs emitted a strong blue luminescence. Using QS as the reference, the QY is determined to be 16 % (Fig. 6h & i). Table 2 presents literature data regarding the QY of PCDs synthesized using various methods [5,70–77]. The fluorescence characteristics of PCDs are impacted by their surface chemistry, notably the presence of “defect sites” on the surface, which trap excited-state energy and yield a QY as high as 16 %. Furthermore, fluorescence intensity measurements conducted over time verified the photostability of the PCDs, as they exhibited no notable photobleaching when continuously excited at 390 nm for 10 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent “turn-off” sensing and detection of ferric ions
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the application of the newly synthesized PCDs for fluorescent detection of ferric and other metal ions, solutions of Fe3+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Ag+, and Na+ at a concentration of 250 μM are equilibrated with the carbon dots, and the corresponding PL intensity is measured. The results (Fig. 7 a & b) demonstrate the distinctive ability of Fe3+ ions to completely quench the luminescence of the carbon dots at these concentrations. Since other metal ions have minimal impact on the PL intensity of PCDs, while Fe3+ ions cause a dramatic quenching effect, this suggests specific interactions between the PCDs and Fe3+. As reported in a recent study [78], this quenching may result from the rapid electron transfer from the excited states of the carbon dots to the vacant d orbitals of the Fe3+ ions. Additionally, it is important to note that the carboxyl and amino groups present on the aromatic carbon core of the PCDs are rich in excited state electrons after absorbing radiation, thus serving as the fluorescent centers. These groups interact strongly with the added Fe3+ ions, resulting in the transfer of electron density to the vacant orbitals of iron. This process reduces the deexcitation of the fluorophores via light emission, with the extent of reduction depending on the concentration of Fe3+ ions added.

To further investigate the concentration dependence, studies are conducted by varying the ferric ion concentration from 0 to 250 μM (Fig. 7c). The PL response to different Fe3+ concentrations is analyzed using the differential PL intensity (ΔF = F0 − F, where F0 is the fluorescence intensity of the blank, and F is the intensity in the presence of Fe3+ (Fig. 7d). The results show a strong linear correlation within the concentration range of 0–25 μM (inset in Fig. 7d), with a correlation coefficient of 0.9995, indicating a highly predictable relationship. The limit of detection (LoD) is calculated using the formula LoD = 3σ/S, where σ is the standard deviation of the PL intensities of five blank samples, and S represents the slope of the linear correlation between Fe3+ concentration and the PL response. The calculated LoD is 0.366 μM, which is significantly below the European Union's permissible limit for Fe3+ (3.57 μM/L) [79]. Moreover, this detection limit is lower than many recently reported values for carbon quantum dots used in Fe3+ detection (Table 3 ), indicating the high sensitivity and effectiveness of the method [80–87].

Fig. 8 a-c displays SEM images of PCDs prepared under different conditions, including variations in sonication time, amplitude, and temperature. These images reveal that the particles exhibit a spherical shape and are evenly dispersed across all preparation conditions. The particles are observed to be in the range of 20–30 nm, aligning well with the results obtained from TEM analysis. This consistency between the two imaging techniques reinforces the reliability of the measurements and provides a clearer understanding of the particle size distribution. Such agreement is crucial for validating the characterization of the carbon dots and their potential applications in various fields. EDAX analysis conducted on the PCDs prepared under these diverse conditions indicates that the PCDs primarily consist of C and O with no other impurities detected, as shown in Fig. 8d-f. The elemental mapping results (inset of Fig. 8d-f) validate the existence of C (red) and O (green) elements in the PCDs. These diagrams of elemental distribution confirm the uniform dispersion of heteroatom functionalities, notably oxygen, throughout the carbon structure. The microscopic characteristics of the PCDs are assessed through TEM analysis, as depicted in Fig. 8g-i. The findings suggest that the particles are spherical, evenly dispersed, and devoid of aggregation. The estimated particle size falls within the range of 5 to 8 nm, with the size distribution histogram (Fig. 8j-l) revealing a narrow distribution of PCDs, thereby confirming the consistent size of the PCDs. The HR-TEM images (Fig. 8m-o) reveal clear lattice fringes with a lattice spacing ranging between 0.231 and 0.236 nm, corresponding to the (100) lattice planes of the PCDs, indicating a high level of crystallinity in the synthesized PCDs. Furthermore, the d-spacing is calculated using Fast Fourier Transform (FFT). The obtained d-spacing values of 0.231, 0.233, and 0.236 nm between adjacent lattice planes correspond to the (100) plane (inset of Fig. 7m-o), aligning well with the HR-TEM findings [88].

Nowadays, the transparency of UV blocking agents or films is essential for various applications. In response to this issue, we fabricated ultra-transparent, flexible, and thin PCDs@PVA NC films with UV-blocking capabilities by integrating waste-derived fluorescent PCDs into biocompatible and biodegradable PVA. The photographic images in Fig. 9 a demonstrate the transparency of both PVA and PCDs@PVA NC films as the concentration of PCDs increases (0.015––0.120 wt%). These images demonstrate that the fabricated composite films maintain high transparency under both daylight and 365 nm UV light. Even with increasing PCDs concentrations up to 0.120 wt%, the transparency of the PCDs@PVA NC films remains largely unaffected. Additionally, the flexibility of these NCs is showcased through various folding scenarios, including single fold, double fold, multiple folds, twisting, and rolling, as shown in Fig. 9b. Finally, a stability test is carried out to assess the luminous intensity of the PCDs NC films over a duration spanning from day 1 to day 360, as illustrated in Fig. 9c. The fluorescence intensity of the films exhibits a vivid blue emission even after 360 days of aging. As depicted in Fig. 9c1, there is a discernible decrease in intensity by the 180th day, although the reduction is not significant. Following the 270th day, the light intensity stabilizes, exhibiting only a minor decline from the initial day until the end of the 360th day, remaining above 92.53 %. This consistent emission intensity over time underscores the long-term stability of the film and its potential for prolonged utilization across various applications, particularly in AC applications. The photo-stability of PCDs@PVA is crucial for practical use. In Fig. 9d, we present the fluorescence emission spectra of PCDs@PVA solutions at different UV exposure times (0–200 min) as shown in Fig. 9d1. Notably, the fluorescence intensities of the PCDs@PVA composite films remain nearly unchanged throughout the 0–200 min of UV irradiation. These minimal fluctuations in intensity highlight the long-term durability of the PCDs@PVA composite films. To evaluate the mechanical durability of our flexible films incorporating 0.120 wt% PCDs@PVA, mechanical loads ranging from 0 to 5 tons are applied for 20 min each, as depicted in Fig. 9e. This testing regimen allowed us to examine how the films withstand varying levels of mechanical stress, providing valuable insights into their robustness and suitability for practical applications. The films demonstrated consistent performance under both UV 365 nm light and standard lighting conditions when subjected to the release of mechanical stress, highlighting their exceptional mechanical durability. This durability makes them highly suitable for various applications, including solid-state lighting and traffic signalling. Additionally, their robustness ensures reliability and longevity in demanding environments, enhancing their utility in diverse settings beyond traditional illumination. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 9e1, FL tests are carried out to analyze the distribution of PL intensity in blue-emitting flexible films under various mechanical strains (0–5 tons). A consistent distribution of PL intensity is observed across all films when PL is mapped within the region delineated by the black-dotted circle under a 365 nm excitation wavelength. This uniformity suggests homogeneous fluorescence emission properties throughout the films, indicating their uniformity in composition and quality. As depicted in Fig. 9e1, the change in PL intensity with applied load distinctly shows that even with up to 4 tons of load, the PL intensity remains at 95.21 %. These results indicate that the films are well-suited for practical use in flexible displays, where flexibility is crucial for optimal performance.

Understanding the thermal decomposition characteristics is crucial for analysing the combustion behaviour of polymeric materials [89]. Fig. S6 a & b depict the thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and derivative thermogravimetric (DTG) curves for both PVA and its composite films, respectively. The corresponding thermal data are detailed in Table 4 . As illustrated in Fig. S6 a, all films underwent two stages of degradation. This data aids in assessing the thermal stability and decomposition profiles of the composite films, offering insights into their performance under different temperature conditions. In the case of the pristine PVA film, the majority of weight loss is observed between 195 and 410 °C. The degradation process initiated with the elimination of side groups, resulting in significant releases of water and the generation of small, combustible molecules like aldehydes, ketones, and benzene. In the subsequent phase, the film underwent a transformation, acquiring a conjugated structure that conferred improved thermal stability. This phase involved additional cross-linking with the cyclized structure. During the second stage, spanning from 410-500 °C, the predominant processes are random chain scission and cyclization [90]. Within the PCDs@PVA composite films, a multitude of surface functional groups such as –NH2, –OH, COOH, and PVA molecular side groups underwent elimination during the initial phase (180–250 °C). In the corresponding DTG curve ( Fig. S6 b), with the increase in PCDs content, the T1 temperature of the composite films shows a gradual decrease. This phenomenon is attributed to the heightened presence of side groups and phosphoric acid in the composite film, indicating that the incorporation of the PCDs solution promotes the pyrolysis of PVA. The second stage takes place between 370 and 470 °C, during which the primary reactions involve crosslinking, chain scission, and cyclization of the PVA chains. At this juncture, the thermal decomposition temperature of all composite films aligns with that of pure PVA, with the T2 temperature increasing as the PCDs solution content rises (Table 4). This demonstrates that adding PCDs solution effectively enhances the thermal stability of PVA.

The typical stress–strain behaviours for PVA and 0.015–0.120 wt% PCDs@PVA are shown in Fig. S7 . Table 5 lists the obtained mechanical properties, which include toughness (T), elongation at break (εb), ultimate tensile strength (σs), and Young's modulus (Y). The PVA mix has the following properties: an ultimate tensile strength (σs) of 61 ± 7 MPa, an elongation at break (εb) of 40 ± 2 %, a toughness (T) of 1480 ± 103 J/m3, and a Young's modulus (Y) of 925 ± 155 MPa. These results for the blend, compared to pure PVA polymers [91,92], suggest the creation of linkages between the chains of blend components, increasing the network structure and consequently modifying the tensile characteristics of the polymeric matrix. Furthermore, these values are consistent with previous reports [93–95].

The PCDs@PVA NCs films demonstrated a notable improvement in tensile properties, notably in Y, T and σs. This enhancement can be ascribed to the establishment of an additional bonded network resulting from the incorporation of PCDs into the polymer blend [96,97]. This network is formed through hydrogen bonding and strong electrostatic interactions between the PVA blend and PCDs. With the addition of 0.120 wt% CDs, the values of Y and σs show significant increases, rising by 144 % and 83 %, respectively. Y increases from 925 ± 155 to 2261 ± 137 MPa, while ultimate tensile strength rises from 61 ± 7 to 1126 ± 9 MPa. These improvements demonstrate that PCDs@PVA films possess remarkable. Additionally, the toughness of these filled films is higher; for the 0.120 wt% PCDs@PVA sample, the maximum value is 2679 ± 163 J/m3. The filling procedure has no discernible effect on the NC elongation at break (εb); nevertheless, a decrease is noted, going from 40 ± 2 % for pure PVA to 30 ± 1 % for 0.120 wt% PCDs@PVA. The slight decrease in εb, coupled with the continuous rise in T computed for the prepared NC films, suggests that these samples possess mechanical flexibility. Moreover, ensuring the uniform dispersion of PCDs with appropriate interfacial adhesion is crucial for enhancing the ultimate tensile properties of PCDs@PVA NCs films [98,99]. In such scenarios, mechanically flexible and resilient bio-NCs materials are attained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV shielding behaviour of the PCDs@PVA NCs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 10 a illustrates a simple experimental setup employed to evaluate the UV shielding effectiveness of the polymer film. A UV digital lux meter is placed directly below (3 cm) the polymer film holder. The light source could either be a UV lamp or natural daylight. A digital lux UV meter is employed to quantify the UV light intensity once it traversed through the polymer film. This setup allows for the accurate measurement of the UV shielding capability of the film under various lighting conditions, aiding in the assessment of its protective properties against harmful UV radiation. Fig. 10b presents a schematic depicting the mechanism of PCDs in the context of UV-blocking film application. As mentioned earlier, PCDs are highly proficient in converting UV light to blue light. When a PCDs solution is placed directly on top of a UV LED torch, the strong blue luminescence emitted from the PCDs solution becomes evident (Fig. 10c). In the experimental setup depicted in Fig. 10c, when a PCDs@PVA film is inserted between the PCDs solution and the UV torch, only a faint blue luminescence is observable. Particularly, with a 0.120 wt% PCDs@PVA NC film, the residual UV light is entirely absorbed, resulting in no luminescence emanating from the PCDs solution. This observation underscores the effective UV blocking capability of the PCDs@PVA film, as it efficiently absorbs and attenuates UV radiation, thereby safeguarding the underlying solution from UV exposure. As the concentration of PCDs in PVA rises, the emitted color gradually weakens. This experiment effectively showcases the UV-blocking capability of PCDs@PVA film, making it highly promising for UV protection applications.

UV–vis absorption and transmittance spectra offer qualitative understanding of the data collected, whereas a UV digital lux meter allows for quantitative analysis to assess the precise UV-blocking efficacy of the polymer films [101]. Hence, we chose a UV digital lux meter for testing to showcase the UV-shielding performance of the polymer films. Initially, when exposed to daylight, the pure PVA film without PCDs blocked only 12.12 % of UV radiation, whereas the 0.015 wt% PCDs@PVA film blocked 65.9 %. As illustrated in Fig. 10d, the UV-shielding capacities of 0.045–0.120 wt% PCDs@PVA films are 87.87 %, 90.60 %, and 95.45 %, respectively. A similar experiment is conducted under daylight conditions for 120 h, with consistent results shown in Fig. 10e. Following 120 h of sunlight exposure, the PVA films blocked only 8.78 % of UV light, while films containing PCDs maintained high UV shielding performance. Specifically, the representative films (0.045–0.120 wt% PCDs@PVA) exhibited optimal UV-shielding capability, blocking more than 94.54 % of UV light [102]. Blocking Value of Pure PVA and PCDs@PVA NCs films UV-A, UV-B, UV-C and HEBL Regions is shown in Table 6
.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV-A/B/C and HEBL blocking performance of PCDs@PVA composite films
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the efficacy of the films in attenuating UV and HEBL radiation, we analyzed the absorption and transmittance spectra of PVA and PCDs@PVA NC films with concentrations ranging from 0.015 to 0.120 wt% (Fig. 11 a & b). This investigation aims to determine the films capability to absorb and transmit specific wavelengths of light, providing insights into its potential as a protective barrier against harmful UV and HEBL radiation. The absorption spectra revealed that the distinctive absorption peak of PCDs at 425 nm became more pronounced with rising PCD concentration (Fig. 11a). This heightened absorption suggests that PCDs can proficiently and selectively absorb and obstruct UV and HEBL radiation. As depicted in the transmission spectra of the films (Fig. 11b), the PVA film devoid of PCDs transmitted 88 % of light at 550 nm, showcasing excellent transparency. With the increment in PCDs concentration, the transmittance of the PCDs@PVA films progressively decreased in the UV and HEBL regions. Table 7 presents the blocking rates for UV-C/B/A and HEBL regions in both the pure PVA film and the PCDs@PVA films with concentrations ranging from 0.015 to 0.120 wt%. The pure PVA film demonstrated blocking rates of 39.6 % for UV-C, 31.3 % for UV-B, 16.7 % for UV-A, and 12.1 % for HEBL. The blocking rates for UV-A, UV-B, UV-C, and HEBL regions in the PCDs@PVA films increased as the PCD content rose. The 0.075 wt% PCDs@PVA film achieved complete blocking of UV-C and UV-B radiation, with rates of 100 %, and nearly complete blocking of UV-A at 99.7 %, along with an impressive 98.2 % blocking of HEBL. Additionally, the 0.120 wt% PCDs@PVA film demonstrated over 99.9 % effectiveness in blocking HEBL and provided 100 % blocking of UV radiation across all regions. In contrast, the pure PVA films did not display any significant absorption peaks (Fig. 11c). The UV and HEBL protection efficacy of the PVA films is significantly lower compared to the PCDs@PVA films (Fig. 11d). A PVA film with a thickness of 100 μm blocked only 11.2 % of UV-A and 9.1 % of HEBL. When the thickness is increased to 200 μm, the blocking rates improved but remained relatively modest, with 25.3 % of UV-A and 17.5 % of HEBL being blocked (Table 7). This contrast highlights the superior protective capabilities of the PCDs@PVA films over the pure PVA films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH-Sensing wearable device fabricated using PCDs@PVA NC
ce:section.ce:para: Owing to its highly stable fluorescence and robust mechanical properties, the PCDs@PVA NC film exhibits significant potential for use in the development of various devices across multiple applications. One innovative application is the creation of a wearable pH detector, which could offer substantial health benefits by monitoring and providing critical information about the wearer's physiological conditions. Human perspiration, which contains lactic acid released by active muscles, generally has a pH range between 4 and 8. Therefore, accurately detecting pH variations in sweat is vital for monitoring physiological health data. Fig. 12 a presents a schematic representation of a wearable pH detector, serving as a proof-of-concept for this innovative application. This device would be capable of intelligently sensing changes in sweat pH, providing valuable insights into an individual's health status and potentially enabling real-time health monitoring and early detection of physiological imbalances. The essential elements of the wearable pH detector comprise detection module, responsive film and UV light emitter and collector. Before deploying the device, the pH responsiveness of the PCDs@PVA film is assessed. This evaluation involves exposing the film to different pH levels and recording its fluorescence spectra in response to these variations (Fig. 12b). This process ensures that the film accurately reflects changes in pH, which is critical for the device's functionality in real-world applications. The data collected from these initial tests confirm the film's sensitivity and effectiveness in detecting pH changes, thereby validating its potential for use in health monitoring devices. As anticipated, the luminous intensity diminished with increasing pH values, confirming the expected pH responsiveness and its relevance in reflecting the pH range of human perspiration. It is noteworthy that despite the presence of PCDs within the PVA, the –OH augmented PVA chains exhibit the ability to stabilize significant quantities of water molecules. This characteristic underscores the film's capacity to effectively function in environments with varying moisture levels, further enhancing its suitability for practical applications. Consequently, a composite film demonstrating remarkably sensitive pH responsiveness is engineered, facilitating the establishment of an artificial ion-migration channel that expedites the rapid transit of various components present in human sweat. Ensuring fluorescence stability is another pivotal aspect of this pH-responsive NC film. Subsequently, the intensity is re-evaluated while regulating pH stimulation to ensure consistent and reliable performance over time. This meticulous assessment of fluorescence stability reinforces the reliability and suitability of films for continuous use in monitoring physiological parameters. In addition, effect of Cl- ions and urea on FL of PCDs@PVA film are discussed in Note S3 of supplementary section ( Fig. S9 ).

Fig. 12c illustrates that the intensities of fluorescence are consistently maintained within a stable range when the pH value remains constant. This observation indicates the film's ability to accurately and reliably detect pH stimulation. Such consistent performance serves as robust validation of the capabilities of the NC films, establishing a strong foundation for the development of the pH detector. Subsequently, a direct assessment of pH variation, as indicated by fluorescence intensity, is carried out in a real-world running scenario. This practical test further verifies the reliability and effectiveness of the pH detector under dynamic conditions, affirming its potential for practical applications in monitoring physiological parameters during physical activity. Fig. 12d showcases the effectiveness of the snugly fitted PCDs@PVA film in real-time detection of pH changes in human sweat. Furthermore, with the prolongation of the running duration, there is a noticeable increase in the relative fluorescence intensity, signifying a decrease in pH value concurrent with sweat secretion. The relative intensity ratio surged to 2.12 after 40 min of operation, highlighting substantial variability in this continuous physiological process. This dynamic response of the pH detector underscores its capability to provide timely and accurate insights into changes in sweat pH levels during physical activity, thereby demonstrating its practical utility in real-world scenarios. We are confident that this wearable pH detector represents a promising solution for accurately monitoring and detecting pH fluctuations in human perspiration during physical activity. With its high performance and pH responsiveness, we envision that this film can be leveraged in wearable intelligent devices, significantly enhancing healthcare services aimed at improving human health and well-being. By providing real-time insights into physiological changes, this technology has the potential to revolutionize how we monitor and manage our health, ultimately leading to more proactive and personalized healthcare interventions [103].

Fig. 13 a & b depict variously shaped luminous hydrogels illuminated under daylight and 365 nm UV light. These luminous hydrogels can maintain their luminescence for up to 360 days (Fig. 13c). PL experiments are conducted on variously shaped hydrogels aged from day 1 to 180 days under 365 nm excitation (Fig. 13e). The unique blue fluorescence of PCDs can be observed in all hydrogels. Additionally, compared to hydrogels aged for 365 days, those aged for day 1 exhibit the highest normalized PL intensity, indicating greater responsiveness to stimuli. These luminescent hydrogels show a broad spectrum of potential applications spanning various fields, including soft robotics, bio-imaging, bio-mimetic actuators, optical display sensing, information encoding, and information encryption [104]. Additionally, the luminous hydrogels are exposed to UV irradiation for different durations, with photographs captured at intervals of 5, 30, 90, and 180 min. This systematic examination allows for a comprehensive understanding of how the hydrogels respond to UV exposure over time, providing valuable insights into their stability and performance characteristics for various applications. As depicted in Fig. 13d, the hydrogel exhibits a very gradual fading process, with the intensity not diminishing entirely even after 90 min of UV exposure. It is only when the illumination time is extended to 180 min that the hydrogel begins to noticeably fade. Furthermore, PL measurements are performed on UV-irradiated luminous hydrogel. These measurements reveal that the slight fading of the hydrogel correlates with an increase in UV illumination time, as illustrated in Fig. 13f.

In recent years, the world has encountered significant challenges stemming from the unauthorized replication of crucial documents or goods including certificates, currency, renowned brands, medications, food items, and more. This pervasive issue has had detrimental repercussions on various fronts, including hindering social development, disrupting the global economy, and posing risks to human health [105]. Efforts are currently being made to curb these activities by imposing stringent regulations in the capital market, while ongoing research endeavours aim to address these challenges through the introduction of enhanced security measures. Among these measures, anti-counterfeiting ink has emerged as a focal point of interest, offering effective protection against a range of unethical practices. In this study, we developed luminescent security ink to assess the suitability of the synthesized phosphor for anti-counterfeiting applications. The formulated PCDs luminescent security ink presents several beneficial characteristics, including the cost-effective availability of the ink medium, efficient dispersion of optimized phosphor within the ink medium, high luminescent intensity, and adhesive properties on diverse surfaces. To assess the versatility of the developed security ink, we applied it to create labels on different surfaces using screen printing, sketching with a pen, and brush application techniques [106]. Fig. 14 a-c showcases anti-counterfeiting labels on a diverse of surfaces, such as paper, glass, marble, ceramic, craft paper, Al. foil, painted wood, and cellulose paper. These labels are created using screen printing, sketching with a pen, and brush application methods.

The images display the appearance of the labels under daylight and UV 365 nm light illumination, demonstrating their effectiveness across various substrates and lighting conditions. As depicted in the figure, it's clear that the anti-counterfeiting patterns and encrypted information designed using the security ink are invisible under normal lighting conditions. However, when illuminated with 365 nm light, the corresponding patterns and encrypted information exhibit a bright red emission, becoming clearly visible. This fluorescence response provides a reliable and easily detectable means of verifying the authenticity of the labelled items, enhancing their security against counterfeiting attempts. Therefore, the synthesized PCDs demonstrate exceptional solubility and dispersion within a PVA medium, rendering them highly suitable as luminescent materials for security ink applications, particularly as anti-counterfeiting markers. Building on the insights gained from the aforementioned observations, it is evident that optimized PCDs hold promise for the development of cost-effective and straightforward security ink solutions. These inks can effectively conceal information and serve as robust deterrents against counterfeiting activities. The developed security ink can find applications beyond anti-counterfeiting measures and may also be employed for printing essential documents, branded products, or prescription labels within the pharmaceutical industry. In comparison to screen printing and sketching with pens, the brush mode technique offers advantages in creating intricate designs. It is notably easier, more straightforward, and cost-effective, making it a preferred choice for implementing detailed and precise patterns or labels with the security ink. This versatility enhances the utility of the ink across various sectors, ensuring efficient and reliable implementation for a range of purposes.

As illustrated in Fig. 14d & e, aging experiments are carried out for a duration of up to 45 days, conducted at both RT and freezing temperature conditions. During these experiments, the fluorescent AC labels affixed to paper displayed a vivid blue glow under UV light at 365 nm for a duration of up to 30 days. However, beyond this period, the fluorescence gradually diminished, indicating a reduction in the longevity of the luminescent effect over time. Even with aging, the AC patterns on paper retained their fluorescence remarkably well under both RT and freezing temperature conditions. These results highlight the robust optical and storage stability of PCDs, showcasing their multifunctionality and reinforcing their suitability for a wide range of applications. The resilience of the patterns, even when applied to a ceramic mould and exposed to temperatures of 100, 200 and 300 ◦C, is evident from only a slight loss in intensity, as demonstrated in Fig. 14f. This underscores the remarkable photo-stability of these luminescent inks, which is crucial for their practical application in various settings. Fig. 14g depicts the AC labels created using a straightforward brush mode technique on paper that has been exposed to a high-intensity UV source (36 mW/cm2) for durations ranging from 10 to 120 min. The results indicate that the fluorescence intensity remains stable for up to 60 min before showing a gradual decline. These findings showcase the potential utility of synthetic PCDs ink for encoding sensitive data with covert security features. Table 8 provides the comparison of security features, security level and stability of anti-counterfeiting ink of present work (PCDs) with reported literature [107–115].

Subsequently, a series of thoughtfully devised multiple encryption strategies are employed to enhance the anti-counterfeiting measures. As depicted in Fig. 15 a, ASCII binary codes are generated utilizing the fluorescent PCDs. The decryption process involves three distinct steps: Initially, it entails distinguishing between the presence of blue fluorescence and the absence of fluorescence when observed under UV light. Following this, the second step involves assigning the blue fluorescence patterns and the absence of fluorescence patterns to the numerical values 1 and 0, respectively. Lastly, the binary codes are translated into the letters “FILTER” based on the ASCII codes, completing the decryption process. This method effectively translates the visual patterns observed under UV light into meaningful alphanumeric characters, offering a simple yet robust approach to data encryption and decryption using fluorescent PCDs. Furthermore, an additional strategy is developed to elevate the level of anti-counterfeiting measures, incorporating more intricate patterns such as high-precision Quick Response (QR) codes. As depicted in Fig. 15b, these QR codes are meticulously crafted using PCDs, allowing them to be scanned by a smartphone equipped with a QR code reader app. Upon scanning, the smartphone retrieves and displays corresponding information, enhancing security and providing users with instant verification capabilities ( https://www.hellotech.com/ ). Under regular visible light conditions, the paper appeared clear and transparent, with no discernible QR code visible. However, upon exposure to UV light at 365 nm, a distinct QR code emerged on the paper containing PCDs. For instance, scanning this QR code led to accessing a website, as depicted in Fig. 15b. This hidden feature enhances the security of the document or product, as the QR code is only revealed under specific lighting conditions, ensuring authenticity verification without compromising the appearance of the item. The revelation of this information solely upon exposure to UV light offers a significant advantage in preventing information leakage. These findings suggest that the implementation of carefully crafted multiple encryption approaches has the potential to elevate the level of anti-counterfeiting measures. Furthermore, such strategies hold promises for applications in anti-counterfeiting packaging, where ensuring the integrity and authenticity of products is of paramount importance. By incorporating covert security features that are only accessible under specific conditions, such as UV light exposure, manufacturers can effectively safeguard their products against counterfeiting attempts and unauthorized access to sensitive information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development of paper-based sensors and their application to actual water samples
ce:section.ce:para: In contrast to conventional detection methods that frequently require costly and precise instruments, PCDs paper-based sensors offer numerous advantages. These include their ease of operation, portability, low cost and rapid detection capabilities [116]. Additionally, PCDs demonstrate a broad detection range for Fe3+ ions, coupled stable fluorescence performance with uniform particle size distribution. These characteristics collectively make PCDs paper-based sensors a highly attractive option for various analytical applications, particularly in settings where cost-effectiveness, simplicity, and speed are paramount considerations. Leveraging the outstanding properties of PCDs, we engineered a PCDs paper-based sensor for the detection of Fe3+. As illustrated in Fig. 16 a, we initially constructed standard cards for Fe3+ detection in the laboratory. Subsequently, we noted that the luminescence intensity of the PCDs paper-based sensor gradually declined with increasing Fe3+ concentration, as depicted in Fig. 16b. This observation highlights the effectiveness of the sensor in detecting Fe3+ ions in water bodies, demonstrating its potential usefulness in environmental monitoring and water quality assessment applications. Following this, we assessed the potential application of paper-based sensors in real-world scenarios by testing them with surface water, wastewater treatment plant effluent, and tap water samples. Given the typically low Fe3+ concentrations in these samples, we supplemented them with 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 μM Fe3+ for testing purposes [117]. Subsequently, the solutions are analyzed using a paper-based sensor, and the outcomes are presented in Fig. 16c. This evaluation provides valuable insights into the sensors performance under practical conditions, demonstrating its effectiveness in detecting Fe3+ ions across various water sources. The detection results obtained from the paper-based sensor align closely with those obtained from the standard card, confirming the sensor's reliability and consistency. Additionally, we conducted practical validation using sludge treatment effluent containing elevated Fe3+ content. As depicted in Fig. 16d, the concentration of Fe3+ in the wastewater is measured to be 45 μM after undergoing three dilutions using the o-phenanthroline colorimetric method [118,119].

This measurement is corroborated by the results obtained from the paper-based sensor, which indicated a Fe3+ content falling within the range of 40 to 50 μM. This practical validation further validates the accuracy and effectiveness of the paper-based sensor in detecting Fe3+ ions in complex environmental samples, highlighting its potential for real-world applications in water quality monitoring and environmental management. The successful application of our paper-based sensor underscores its feasibility and reliability for detecting Fe3+ ions in real-world water bodies. This achievement represents a promising avenue for future developments, particularly in the creation of portable Fe3+ detection test strips. By offering a convenient and efficient solution for on-site Fe3+ detection, these test strips have the potential to revolutionize water quality monitoring efforts, enabling rapid and accurate assessment of Fe3+ levels in various environmental conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visualization of latent fingerprints using PCDs@PVA NC
ce:section.ce:para: Owing to its advantageous FL and excellent solubility characteristics, PCDs are effectively dispersed within the polymer matrix to produce PCDs@PVA NC well-suited for LFP identification. The procedural representation for LFP collection and visualization is depicted in Fig. 2c. A droplet of PCDs@PVA aqueous solution is deposited onto a LFP present on various surfaces. As the solvent evaporated, a freestanding, flexible, transparent film formed, adhering to the surface. This film could then be effortlessly peeled off, unveiling the diverse ridge/furrow details of the LFPs (Fig. 17 a). During this process, the water insolubility of lipid residues presents in FPs played a crucial role. This property prevented any damage to the LFP pattern deposited on the substrate when the PCDs@PVA aqueous solution is added. As a result, the FP pattern remained intact, allowing for its non-destructive pickup by the PCDs@PVA NC. This preservation ensured the fidelity of the FP details, enabling accurate visualization and analysis without compromising the original pattern. Fig. 17b showcases the 3D interactive plots of FPs on various surfaces. These developed FPs exhibit a uniform and clearly defined arrangement of PCDs along the ridges, as evidenced by the 3D surface imaging. This alignment strongly indicates robust binding interactions between the PCDs and FP residues, underscoring the effectiveness of the PCDs@PVA NC film in capturing and visualizing LFPs with exceptional resolution and fidelity.

The structural characteristics of FPs consist of staggered ridges and grooves, which contribute to their distinct patterns. These patterns can be categorized into three levels of detail: typical features (level I), visible features (level II) and micro features (level III). Fig. 17c illustrates the global and local features of FPs on an aluminum foil surface. Right loops belong to level I features, which can provide a rough classification of FPs but cannot determine the basic identity of an individual. Therefore, studying level II FP features is essential. Ridge end, double bifurcation, enclosure, hook, dot, bridge, and island all belong to level II features. Level II features can distinguish the unique characteristics of different individuals because the combination of these visible details is unique to each person. FP scanning machines primarily focus on level II features to determine an individual's identity. Similarly significant, level III features also contribute to distinguishing individual characteristics. Sweat pores are part of level III features, playing a crucial role in identifying individuals when FPs are incomplete or damaged. The contrast of FP displays serves as a valuable metric for evaluating the efficacy of PCDs in FP visualization [120–123].

Fig. 17d illustrates the development of LFP images using the probe after 1 and 365 days of placement. The comparison indicates that even after 7 days, the FP images remain intact. Moreover, the distinct structure of the sweat pores in the FP remains clearly visible in the local features, indicating the robust imaging performance of the probe. Additionally, upon comparing the probe solution after 7 days with the new configuration, it is evident that the probe solution maintains its homogeneity and transparency. There is no statistically significant structural variation in the FPs during imaging, and no observable physical or chemical deterioration is evident. Fig. 17e displays the pixel profiles of a selected area on LFPs aged for 1 to 365 days. Through the analysis of pixel value fluctuations, distinct grooves and ridges are delineated, showcasing the stacking of PCDs along these features. This observation underscores the high-resolution capability of LFP detection achieved with PCDs@PVA. Overall, these findings highlight the promising applications of PCDs@PVA in LFP detection, offering a reliable and efficient method for capturing detailed FP patterns over extended periods of time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology of the developed fingerprints through SEM
ce:section.ce:para: SEM proves invaluable for identifying second-level FP features, such as the core, delta, ridge endings, and bifurcations. In Fig. 18 a, representative developed FPs on glass display these features distinctly. Additionally, third-level features such as pores are also discernible, demonstrating the high-resolution capabilities of SEM in FP analysis. This technique offers a detailed insight into the intricate structure of FPs, enabling comprehensive identification and analysis of individual features for forensic applications. PCDs are selectively electrodeposited on the bare surface between the FP ridges, commonly referred to as valleys. This process yields two compositionally distinct surface regions: the ridges and the intervening spaces. Analysis of SEM images from aged samples provides insights into the decrease in the visual perception of contrast between the FP and the PCDs dispersion over time, as depicted in Fig. 18b & c. This phenomenon can be attributed to various factors, such as the gradual degradation or redistribution of PCDs over the surface, leading to a reduction in the distinctiveness of the FP features. This microscopic insight provides clarity to the macroscopic evaluation of image quality, especially for non-porous substrates. Over time, aging leads to the evaporation of water present in the FP residue, while some organic components degrade or oxidize. As a result, the distinctiveness of FP features may diminish, impacting the visual contrast between the FP ridges and the PCDs dispersion. As a consequence, the “masking” effect becomes less effective, given the reduced inhibition of PCDs deposition on the residue. Fig. 18d & e illustrates the pixel profiles of the selected region on fresh and aged SEM FPs, providing a quantitative analysis of the FP changes based on the red value. Even after aging for 365 days, the red value shows only minor fluctuations, retaining a significant contrast between its peaks and valleys. This indicates that LFP images can remain distinctly discernible even after prolonged aging when presented with the masking effect of PCDs@PVA. With its resilience to aging, PCDs can be more efficiently employed in the field of forensic LFP identification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fps analysis through the deep learning method
ce:section.ce:para:
The training of the YOLOv8x network utilized a curated dataset containing various FP types developed with 0.120 wt% PCDs@PVA. This dataset included bounding box annotations for minutiae features, such as ridge endings and bifurcations. This dataset is meticulously prepared to encompass diverse FP characteristics and patterns. During the training process, the YOLOv8 network effectively learned to detect and identify FP minutiae patterns and features from the input images, leveraging the extensive dataset and manual annotations provided. This training process aimed to enhance the capability of the YOLOv8 network in detecting and recognizing intricate FP details in real-time scenarios. During the training process, we utilized sigmoid activation and rectified linear unit (ReLU) functions across 39 epochs to refine the models, optimizing their performance. The training is conducted on a V100 server equipped with an Intel(R) Core(TM) i5–3470 CPU and 32 GB RAM, ensuring efficient processing and memory utilization. Once trained, the model demonstrated its capability to accurately detect and localize minutia FP characteristics in unseen images. Using convolutional and pooling layers, the algorithm efficiently extracted features from the input FP images. It employed bounding boxes to predict the presence and positions of minutiae points with accuracy. Consequently, the algorithm generated results comprising bounding boxes that encapsulated the minutiae points. This output offers essential spatial minutiae location data, facilitating further processing and analysis of the FP data. This data can be utilized for various applications, including FP matching and verification against known databases, facilitating enhanced forensic analysis and identification processes. Our FP recognition algorithm presented in this study provides a fast and accurate approach to detecting and pinpointing minutia features in FP images. This capability facilitates the implementation of automated FP recognition systems across various fields, such as access control, biometric identification and law enforcement. Certainly, the computational analysis process for FP identification, as illustrated in Fig. 19 , involves several key stages when utilizing object detection methods:
1.
Pre-processing: As depicted in Fig. 19a, the original input FP images undergo pre-processing prior to feature extraction using deep learning. This crucial initial step ensures the accuracy and reliability of the experimental results. Pre-processing techniques are applied to enhance the quality of the images, remove noise, and standardize their characteristics, thereby optimizing their suitability for subsequent analysis. Pre-processing tasks include resizing, normalization, annotation, binarization, and other transformations to ensure compatibility with the model [124]. The objective function evaluates the performance of the deep learning algorithm by minimizing errors through the assessment of the loss function. This function quantifies the disparity between predicted outputs and ground truth values, guiding the optimization process during training to enhance the model's accuracy and effectiveness in FP identification tasks.
2.
Training Model: In the training model phase, pre-processed FP images with pre-trained weights are used to train the model, as depicted in Fig. 19b. These images share similar characteristics, such as image size ratio and object size, but vary in illumination due to their creation with a 0.120 wt% PCDs@PVA solution. This diversity in illumination levels ensures that the model is robust and capable of accurately identifying and classifying FPs under varying lighting conditions, enhancing its applicability in real-world scenarios. Utilizing convolutional neural networks (CNNs), the model identifies features within FP images [125]. This computational analysis process includes pre-processing and training using the model, ensuring accurate and comprehensive feature recognition within FP images. Despite variations in illumination and the solutions used in their creation, this approach ensures robust and reliable performance in identifying key FP features. This capability is crucial for various applications, including forensic analysis and biometric identification, where precise feature recognition is essential for accurate results.
3.
Prediction: Indeed, during the prediction phase of YOLOv8x, the refinement of bounding box coordinates and the filtering of low-confidence detections take place through post-processing steps, as illustrated in Fig. 19c. These steps are typically outlined as follows:
a.
Bounding Box Refinement: Initially, the predictions include bounding boxes, class probabilities, and confidence scores [126,127]. These predictions are then refined and adjusted to improve their alignment with the detected objects. This refinement process aims to increase the accuracy and precision of the object detection, ensuring that the identified features correspond more closely to the actual characteristics of the FP minutiae.
b.
Handling Overlapping Boxes: To mitigate redundancy arising from overlapping bounding boxes, non-maximum suppression (NMS) is employed. This method identifies the most confident and reliable detections while eliminating redundant or overlapping boxes.
c.
Final Output: The results generated by the YOLOv8x analysis offer an extensive catalog of identified FP characteristics, accompanied by corresponding bounding boxes and class labels. These refined predictions and data are versatile, capable of undergoing additional processing, visualization, or deployment in subsequent tasks such as object tracking, recognition, or classification. This flexibility enhances the adaptability and utility of the analysis outcomes across various applications and domains. Through refining the bounding boxes and prioritizing the most confident detections, these post-processing steps ensure that the final output is optimized for accuracy and effectiveness in downstream applications. Fig. 19d shows FPs developed using the optimized 0.120 wt% PCDs@PVA solution on glass surfaces, captured under 365 nm UV light. Utilizing FP images directly in deep learning for dimensionality reduction and feature extraction significantly improves experimental accuracy. While the existing FP preparation technique nears the ideal, we introduce an innovative YOLOv8x embedded deep learning model for rapid feature recognition. This model treats the entire FP as a unified entity to predict bounding box coordinates. Our YOLOv8x, powered by a deep convolutional neural network (DCNN), identifies bifurcation and ridge end features, as illustrated in Fig. 19e-h. The model undergoes training for 39 epochs for bifurcation and 49 epochs for ridge end detection, utilizing labelled input FPs. Within the model, each region operates on a 7 × 7 grid, predicting five bounding boxes. This methodology enables efficient detection and classification of FP features, thereby improving the accuracy and reliability of the identification process. By employing binarization during pre-processing, as demonstrated in Fig. 19e & g, and employing conditional probability-based bounding box determination with a confidence score threshold of 0.3 via logistic regression, the proposed YOLOv8x model demonstrates exceptional speed and efficiency. This approach enhances the model's ability to accurately detect and classify FP features, while maintaining rapid processing capabilities, making it suitable for real-time applications in various contexts, including forensic analysis and biometric identification.

The results presented in Figs. S10 a-f & S11a-f (Supporting Information) across different epochs facilitate a comparison between the performance of the modified method and the baseline approach (the original YOLOv8x). Improved precision and recall curves, approaching unity, often signify advancements in FP identification techniques. These curves provide valuable insights into the model's capacity to precisely identify pertinent features while minimizing false positives and false negatives, thus enhancing its overall effectiveness in FP recognition tasks. Initially, the precision, recall, and mAP (mean Average Precision) curves for our YOLOv8x model exhibit values hovering around 0.7, indicating areas for enhancement. However, discernible enhancements in detection performance are apparent with the use of the enhanced method. This improvement underscores the effectiveness of the modifications made to the model, showcasing its capacity to more accurately identify and classify FP features. Figs. S10 e-f & S11e-f (Supporting Information) depict the box loss (box_loss), classification loss (cls_loss), and distribution focal loss (dfl_loss) of the enhanced FP-YOLO base model after 39 and 49 training iterations, respectively. These figures also present the detection outcomes of the YOLOv8x model on the validation set. These visualizations provide valuable insights into the training process and model performance, helping to assess the effectiveness of the modifications made to the FP-YOLO model compared to the baseline YOLOv8x model. The improved model exhibits an expanded feature range, reduced rates of missed and false detections, and higher result confidence. This responsiveness across various confidence levels is outlined in Figs. S12 a, c-d, S13a, c-d ( Supporting Information ), which display precision, F1, and recall curves, using a confidence level of 1 as a benchmark for optimal model performance. These visualizations offer comprehensive insights into the model's performance across different confidence thresholds, showcasing its reliability and effectiveness in FP feature detection and classification tasks. Additionally, Figs. S12 b & S13 b present the PR (precision-recall) curve for the ridge end and bifurcation dataset using the YOLOv8x model, alongside the Precision, Recall, and F1 curves. In summary, the visualization of FPs under 365 nm UV light underscores the role of deep learning in enhancing experimental accuracy by reducing dimensionality and extracting features. The YOLOv8x model exhibits notable accuracy in FP visualization, particularly with the 0.120 wt% PCDs@PVA, offering improved precision and contrast compared to alternative methods. While current FP preparation methods are effective, there's a rising demand for a novel real-time feature identification approach. Our suggested YOLOv8x embedded deep learning model meets this need by swiftly predicting bounding box coordinates and class probabilities with high accuracy. Moreover, our DCNN achieves outstanding accuracy in predicting bifurcation and ridge end features, aided by thorough training and pre-processing methods like binarization. These methods ensure reliable fault detection and efficient performance, enhancing the models reliability and effectiveness in FP feature identification. The precision and box dependency results validate the model's efficacy in identifying bifurcation and ridge end features accurately. This study presents a robust approach to FP analysis, paving the way for enhanced identification methods and advancements in forensic science applications [128,129].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Food packaging via dip coating method
ce:section.ce:para: To begin, the green apples undergo washing and drying procedures. Next, the washed green apples are coated twice with PVA solution, followed by application of PCDs, PCDs@PVA solution using the dip-coating technique [130–132]. Subsequently, both the untreated and coated green apples are hung on a specially designed apparatus for air-drying at RT (25 °C) ( Fig. 20 a). Photographs of the green apples are captured using a camera over a period of 0–25 days for visual assessment. The experiment is conducted with a minimum of four repetitions. Green apples, a typical example of perishable fruit, serve as the experimental model for assessing the efficacy of PCDs@PVA based films in prolonging the shelf life of food. The changes in appearance, surface colour, weight loss, and firmness of the green apples before and after storage are depicted in Fig. 20b-e. Fig. 20b-d reveal that both the uncoated and PCDs and PVA coated green apples started to exhibit browning and signs of rotting by the 10th day of storage, with these attributes worsening as time progressed. A similar observation is made with the PCDs@PVA coated green apples after 25 days of storage, as shown in Fig. 20e. Different levels of mixed bacterial infections are detected either at the top or bottom of the green apples, indicating potential microbial contamination. However, green apples coated with the PCDs@PVA maintained a desirable appearance under the same storage conditions, showing only the development of black spots and shrinkage on the surface after 25 days. This underscores the positive influence of the PCDs@PVA visible-light-driven antibacterial functional films in prolonging green apples storage. Additionally, significant weight loss is noted in the untreated (without PVA and PCDs) of green apples during storage, primarily due to moisture evaporation and green apples respiration. Green apples enclosed within the PCDs@PVA exhibited the slowest softening rate, credited to the preservation of cell wall materials and the regulation of softening enzyme activity facilitated by the PCDs@PVA NCs. These findings indicate that the developed PCDs@PVA visible-light-driven antibacterial NCs effectively shield green apples from microbial infections, thereby extending their shelf life.

To evaluate wound dressing blood compatibility, haemolysis is a reliable technique. National safety standards mandate a haemolysis rate below 5 % for haemostatic materials used in organisms. In this study, a haemolysis test is conducted to estimate the haemolysis ratio of PVA and 0.015–0.120 wt% PCDs NC, with the results presented in Fig. 21 a & b. The incorporation of PCDs led to a decrease in the haemolysis rate of the composite. Notably, the haemolysis rate for PCDs@PVA is significantly lower than 5 %, meeting the requirements of national safety standards. This lower haemolysis rate indicates better blood compatibility and reduced red blood cell (RBC) damage. Based on these findings, the NCs film demonstrates suitable blood compatibility, making it a promising material for various applications [104]. Whole blood coagulation is a traditional technique utilized to assess the coagulation ability of haemostatic materials. A lower Blood Clotting Index (BCI) indicates faster coagulation. In this study, a blood clotting test is conducted to evaluate the haemostatic potential of the PCDs@PVA and PVA NC. The results demonstrated that the PCDs@PVA NCs exhibited superior blood clotting capability compared to PVA alone. This enhancement in haemostatic potential can be attributed to the morphological characteristics, micro-structure, and chemical composition of the PCDs@PVA NC. The incorporation of PCDs further improves the blood-clotting potential of the PVA dressing, as PCDs have the ability to influence the intrinsic blood coagulation pathway and denature anticoagulant proteins [133].

Assessing the cytotoxicity or biocompatibility of PCDs in cell lines holds great significance for its biomedical applications. To investigate the interaction between cells and PCDs, SH-SY-5Y cells are employed in an MTT assay experiment to assess the cell viability of PCDs. The cell viability analysis is conducted while the cells are cultured in various concentrations of the PCDs solution (0.015–0.120 µM) for 72 h (Fig. 21c). Following a 72 h incubation period, the corresponding cytotoxicity values are observed as 77, 69, 53, 42, and 36 % for the respective concentrations of PCDs. Furthermore, the calculated IC50 value is determined to be 0.1896 µM. Based on this result, the cytotoxicity effect of PCDs is moderate at higher concentrations against SH-SY-5Y cell lines which suggests that PCDs can be a good candidate for biomedical applications. Biocompatibility is a critical requirement for wound dressing materials. To assess this, the viability of SH-SY-5Y (Neuroblastoma) cell lines in contact with PCDs@PVA and PVA is indirectly quantified using the MTT assay and compared to the controls. The results, depicted in Fig. 21d, revealed that the positive controls exhibited the highest absorption following 72 h of incubation. Interestingly, at the initial stage and after 72 h of incubation, the cell viability for the 0.015–0.120 wt% PCDs@PVA NCs decreased compared to PVA. However, with the 0.120 wt% PCDs@PVA NCs, there is a noticeable enhancement in cell viability by approximately 96.4 % after 48 h. Even after 72 h, although the cell viability decreased with increasing CDs content in the dressing, it remained significantly higher than 95.2 %. These results suggest that both the 0.120 wt% PCDs@PVA composites exhibit no cytotoxicity towards SH-SY-5Y cells. The robust biocompatibility observed can likely be attributed to the excellent properties of PVA and the composite structure. As per the ISO-10993–5-2009 criteria, a cytotoxic substance is one that allows less than 70 % of cells exposed to it for 72 h to survive.

A novel, one-step procedure has been devised for the production of highly fluorescent PCDs utilizing a sonochemical process. This method leverages sonication, where collapsing gas bubbles within the liquid serve as micro-reactors, facilitating transient conditions of extreme temperature and pressure conducive to PEG medium decomposition and CDs formation. By adjusting parameters such as sonication power, duration of ultrasonic irradiation, or reaction temperature, the yield of PCDs can be significantly enhanced. The green emission of PCDs is greatly quenched in the presence of Fe3 + ions as they are able to transfer electrons from the excited fluorophores at the surface into the vacant d orbitals of iron. The PL response of N PCDs is linear with up to 25 μM concentration of Fe3+ and showed a very good LoD value of 0.366. Promising results are obtained when the applicability of newly developed PCDs as highly sensitive fluorescent “turn-off” probe for the detection of Fe3+ ions is tested with river, laboratory and tap waters. Subsequently, the desired PCDs@PVA NC film is prepared via a solution casting technique. The resulting film exhibits a uniform distribution of PCDs within the PVA matrix, attributed to hydrogen bonding interactions between PCDs and PVA chains. Incorporating PCDs into PVA yields flexible and transparent films suitable as ink-free substrates for collecting and identifying LFPs. This approach offers distinct advantages: the materials are readily available, non-toxic, and enable non-destructive retrieval and recognition of long-term exposed LFPs from various surfaces. Moreover, the PCDs@PVA NC film demonstrates effective UV protection upon UV light exposure. Additionally, the UV-blocking PCDs@PVA film maintains transparency and flexibility while preserving the inherent properties of PCDs. This film can be easily prepared by directly mixing PCDs and PVA solutions, eliminating the need for additional treatment or purification. The development of an intelligent pH-detector using the pH-responsive film serves as a compelling demonstration of its practical application in real-time sensing and monitoring of pH variations in human sweat during physical activity, thereby enhancing healthcare services. This work not only presents an environmentally friendly method for producing highly stable and strongly fluorescent materials but also opens up promising avenues for the accurate sensing and detection capabilities of wearable intelligent devices. Additionally, the PCDs@PVA solution coating enhances shelf life by reducing fungal growth, spoilage, and moisture loss at room temperature. Mechanical tests indicate an improvement in the flexibility of the PCDs@PVA NC film, while increased incorporation of PCDs correlates with enhanced cytotoxicity, hemostatic and blood-clotting capabilities, highlighting the excellent cytocompatibility and hemocompatibility of the PCDs@PVA NC.
